name: HIIT improved older-adult mitochondrial respiration and proteome markers
summary: A supervised human exercise-training study supplies the strongest functional and mitochondrial adaptation signal for this track.
statement: In a 12-week exercise-training study, high-intensity interval training and combined training improved aerobic capacity in older adults, while HIIT increased older-adult skeletal-muscle mitochondrial respiration, mitochondrial protein synthesis, and mitochondrial protein abundance.
population_or_model: Sedentary older adults aged 65 to 80 compared with young adults
quantitative_note: The source reports older-adult relative VO2 peak gains of about 17% with HIIT and 21% with combined training, and maximal mitochondrial respiration increased about 69% in older adults after HIIT.
caveats: The study was supervised and enrolled relatively healthy sedentary adults, not frail or multimorbid older adults.
Exercise modality and intensity mattered; resistance training did not significantly increase mitochondrial respiration.
The result supports exercise-driven mitochondrial adaptation, not a druglike biogenesis therapy.
